 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(iii) exit from the digital TV ASSP SoC market and reduced presence in
standalone display port components (furthermore, in February 2014, we entered into a small transaction transferring a part of our display port standalone product portfolio to a third party which hired some employees engaged in this activity).
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Imaging, BiCMOS ASIC&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Silicon Photonics (IBP) </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The IBP Group&#146;s activities focus on developing innovative, competitive and low power solutions and systems for BiCMOS
ASIC and Silicon Photonics as well as Imaging products. For BiCMOS ASIC&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Silicon Photonics, we are focused on developing products for RF, Optical ICs and Silicon Photonics. For Imaging products, we address image capture and processing
product requirements for various segments of the industry, due to four product families, CMOS image sensors, CMOS photonic sensors, Imaging modules and Imaging processors. In the mobile imaging segment, wireless handset and tablets, we produce <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">CMOS-based</FONT> camera modules, CMOS image sensors and processors for low and high density pixel resolutions, which also meet the autofocus, advanced fixed focus and miniaturization requirements of this market.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">IBP&#146;s 2013 developments include: </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(i)&nbsp;for our high volume image sensor business: high volume innovative image sensors using ST&#146;s proprietary <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">backside-illumination</FONT> (&#147;BSI&#148;) technology to camera integrators; our delivering <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">high-value</FONT> dedicated Image Signal Processor (&#147;ISP&#148;) to a leading consumer
brand; and our winning a slot for the ISP from a leading phone manufacturer; </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(ii)&nbsp;for silicon photonics:
securing design wins for ASICs using silicon photonics with two&nbsp;of the world&#146;s top optical communications manufacturers; collecting a broad range of design wins from many customers that use ST BiCMOS or silicon photonics process technology
in almost 30 new ASIC projects; and ramping production for 100G and parallel optics applications; and </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(iii)&nbsp;with regard to diversification: sampling and demonstrating to automotive market leaders a new <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">high-performance</FONT> ISP and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">image-sensor</FONT> chipset with advanced features for automotive and security applications. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Microcontrollers, Memory&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Security (MMS) </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">MMS activities focus on microcontrollers dedicated to General purpose and Secure applications. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">With our STM32 product family, we offer a wide range of 32-bit General Purpose Microcontrollers suitable for a wide
variety of applications from those where a minimum cost is a primary requirement to those that need powerful <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">real-time</FONT> performance and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">high-level</FONT> language support. In
addition, our ST33/31 secure microcontrollers are also based on 32-bit architecture that securely store data and provide an array of security capabilities including advanced data encryption.&nbsp;Our expertise in security is a key to our leadership
in the banking, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">pay-TV,</FONT> mobile communication, identity, and transport fields.&nbsp;We also actively contribute to the emergence of new applications such as secure mobile transactions on near field
communication (&#147;NFC&#148;) mobile phones, trusted computing, brand protection, etc.&nbsp;In addition under the &#147;Incard&#148; brand, we develop, manufacture and sell smartcards for banking, identification and telecom applications.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">MMS is also involved in the memories (EEPROM), which are used for parameter storage in various electronic
devices in all market segments.&nbsp;RF EEPROM memories are opening many new opportunities everywhere.</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">These
products are manufactured in processes capable of embedding nonvolatile memories as appropriate. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">During 2013,
we continued to expand our leadership in <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">32-bit</FONT> by receiving several design wins, such as: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:6px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We also expanded our secure microcontroller business: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:6px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<TD WIDTH="5%"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="2%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&#149;</FONT></P></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">achieved <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Bank-Card</FONT> Test Center certification for our secure microcontroller for Dual Interface Banking in
China. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Wireless (WPS) </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The Wireless Product Line resulted from the combination of our wireless business with NXP&#146;s and with Ericsson&#146;s
mobile platform business to form a joint venture, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> (JVS). </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Upon our decision to exit <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> and break up the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> business (legacy products went to ST and the LTE modem to Ericsson), we report the revenues of phased out <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> products in the Wireless Product Line.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Alliances with Customers and Industry Partnerships </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We believe that alliances with customers and industry partnerships are critical to success in the semiconductor industry.
Customer alliances provide us with valuable systems and application <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">know-how</FONT> and access to markets for key products, while allowing our customers to share some of the risks of product development with us and
to gain access to our process technologies and manufacturing infrastructure. We are actively working to expand the number of our customer alliances, targeting OEMs in the United States, in Europe and in Asia. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Partnerships with other semiconductor industry manufacturers permit costly R&amp;D and manufacturing resources to be
shared to mutual advantage for joint technology development. For example, we belong to the International Semiconductor Development Alliance to <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">co-develop</FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">32/28-nm</FONT> and
below process technologies. In addition, we collaborate closely with the CEA Leti in both process development and design, with recent focus on our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">FD-SOI</FONT> derivative technology. Furthermore, we have joint
development programs with leading suppliers such as Air Liquide, ASM Lithography, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Hewlett-Packard,</FONT> PACKTEC, JSR, SOITEC, Statchip, Teradyne and with electronic design automation (&#147;EDA&#148;) tool
producers, including Cadence, Mentor and Synopsys. We also participate in joint European research programs, such as the ITEA, the Cluster for Application and Technology Research in Europe on NanoElectronics (&#147;CATRENE&#148;), ARTEMIS and the
European Nanoelectronics Initiative Advisory (&#147;ENIAC&#148;) programs. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Customers and Applications </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We design, develop, manufacture and market thousands of products that we sell to thousands of customers. Our major
customers include Apple, Blackberry, Bosch, Cisco, Conti, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Hewlett-Packard,</FONT> Nokia, Oberthur, Samsung, and Western Digital. To many of our key customers we provide a wide range of products, including <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">application-specific</FONT> products, discrete devices, memory products and programmable products. Our broad range portfolio helps foster close relationships with customers, which provides opportunities to supply such
customers&#146; requirements for multiple products, including discrete devices, programmable products and memory products. We also sell our products through distributors and retailers, including Arrow Electronics, Avnet, Wintech and Yosun. The
semiconductor industry has historically been cyclical and we have responded by emphasizing balance in our product portfolio, in the applications we serve and in the regional markets we address. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">No customer exceeded 10% of our total net revenues in both 2013 and 2012. There can be no assurance that our customers or
distributors will continue to place orders with us in the future at the same levels as in prior periods. See &#147;Item&nbsp;3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors &#151; Risks Related to Our Operations &#151; Disruptions in our relationships with
any one of our key customers, and/or material changes in their strategy or financial condition, could adversely affect our results of operations&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Sales, Marketing and Distribution </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In 2012, we reorganized
our Sales&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Marketing organization with the primary objectives of accelerating sales growth and gaining market share. The changes were designed along three&nbsp;key drivers: strengthening the effectiveness of the development of our
global accounts; boosting demand creation through an enhanced focus on geographical coverage; and establishing marketing organizations in our regional sales organizations that are fully aligned with the Product Groups. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">26 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Following this reorganization, the previous sales organization structured by
market segment was replaced by a new sales organization organized by a combination of country/area coverage and key accounts coverage. Our Sales&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Marketing organization is now structured into six units: four regional sales
organizations and two&nbsp;major accounts units. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Regional Sales Organizations </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our four regional sales organizations, a description of which follows below, have a similar structure to enhance
coordination in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">go-to-market</FONT></FONT> activities. They are also strongly focused on accelerated growth. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I></I>(i)&nbsp;<I>EMEA</I> &#151; In EMEA, there are seven sales organizations. Four are geographically defined and
cover North, Central, West and South&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Emerging Markets. Three&nbsp;sales units have worldwide responsibility for global sales of three&nbsp;Global Key Accounts. Marketing is organized to reflect the product groups, representing APG,
Digital, MMS and AMS/IPD. Combined, these organizations are collectively responsible for new and existing account development, technical support and logistics and services support. We also have an organization that manages our distribution network
and supports EMS customers for manufacturing on behalf of our OEM customers.<I> </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I></I>(ii)&nbsp;<I>Americas</I> &#151; In the Americas region, the sales and marketing team is organized into seven
major accounts: Global Key Accounts, Four New Major Accounts, Sales by Geography consisting of the West Coast, Central South, North Central and East Coast Sales. We also have a sales team supporting Latin America based in two&nbsp;centers in Mexico
and Brazil. Our Marketing teams that support and promote specific products are organized in line with our product groups, of which there are six: APG, AMS, DCG, IBP, IPD and MMS. We also have an organization that manages our distribution network and
supports EMS customers mostly for manufacturing on behalf of our OEM customers.<I> </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I></I>(iii)&nbsp;<I>Greater <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">China-South</FONT> Asia</I> &#151; The Greater China-South
Asia region comprises six geographical sales units with offices covering North China (Beijing), Central China (Shanghai), South China (Hong Kong), Taiwan (Taipei), India (New Delhi) and ASEAN/Australia&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;New Zealand (Singapore). It is
further supported by a centralized Channel coordination function, as well as six key product groups, namely, DCG, IBP, APG, IPD, AMS and MMS, and four new major accounts. In 2013, the company also opened 7&nbsp;new offices (6 in mainland China and 1
in Taiwan) in the region.<I> </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(iv)&nbsp;<I><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Japan-Korea</FONT></I> &#151;
The <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Japan-Korea</FONT> region comprises three&nbsp;geographical sales units with offices covering East Japan (Tokyo and Nagoya), West Japan (Osaka), Korea <B>(</B>Seoul) and four new major accounts. It is further
supported by key product groups, namely, DCG/IBP, APG, IPD/AMS and MMS plus a comprehensive Sales Channel Management that provides products and sales support for the regional distribution network. Each geographical sales unit sells each product from
our portfolio that fits the applications. Marketing and Application organization provides product support and training for standard products for the region. In addition, five central support functions (business management, field quality, human
resources, finance and corporate communications) allow the region to run all of the necessary tasks smoothly. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The sales and marketing activities performed by our regional sales organizations are supported by product marketing that
is carried out by each product group, which also includes product development functions. This matrix system reinforces our sales and marketing activities and our broader strategic objectives. An important component of our regional sales and
marketing efforts is to expand our customer base, which we seek to do by adding sales representatives, regional competence centers and new generations of electronic tools for customer support. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We also engage distributors and representatives to distribute our products around the world. Typically, distributors
handle a wide variety of products, including products that compete with our products, and fill orders for many customers. Most of our sales to distributors are made under agreements allowing for price protection and/or the right of return on unsold
merchandise. We generally recognize revenues upon the transfer of ownership of the goods at the contractual point of delivery. Sales representatives generally do not offer products that compete directly with our products, but may carry complementary
items manufactured by others. Representatives do not maintain a product inventory. Their customers place large quantity orders directly with us and are referred to distributors for smaller orders. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">At the request of certain of our customers, we also sell and deliver our products to EMS, which, on a contractual basis
with our customers, incorporate our products into the application specific products they </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">27 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">
manufacture for our customers. Certain customers require us to hold inventory on consignment in their hubs and only purchase inventory when they require it for their own production. This may lead
to delays in recognizing revenues, as revenue recognition will occur, within a specific period of time, at the actual withdrawal of the products from the consignment inventory, at the customer&#146;s option. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">For a breakdown of net revenues by product segment and geographic region for the last three&nbsp;fiscal years, see
&#147;Item&nbsp;5. Operating and Financial Review and Prospects&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Research and Development </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We believe that market driven R&amp;D founded on leading edge products and technologies is critical to our success. The
main R&amp;D challenge we face is continually increasing the functionality, speed and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">cost-effectiveness</FONT> of our semiconductor devices, while ensuring that technological developments translate into profitable
commercial products as quickly as possible. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We combine <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT>
manufacturing and technology R&amp;D under the same organization for each of SP&amp;A and EPS to ensure a smooth flow of information between the R&amp;D and manufacturing organizations and we leverage on significant synergies and shared activities
between the two segments to cross-fertilize both businesses. We manage our R&amp;D projects by technology and by product segment. The relevant technology R&amp;D expenses are allocated to the product segments on the basis of the estimated efforts.
The total amount of R&amp;D expenses in the past three&nbsp;fiscal years was $1,816&nbsp;million, $2,413&nbsp;million and $2,352&nbsp;million in 2013, 2012 and 2011, respectively. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We devote significant effort to R&amp;D because we believe such investment can be leveraged into competitive advantages.
New developments in semiconductor technology can make end products significantly cheaper, smaller, faster, more reliable and embedded with more functionalities than their predecessors. They also enable, through their timely appearance on the market,
significant value creation opportunities. For a description of our R&amp;D expenses, see &#147;Item&nbsp;5. Operating and Financial Review and Prospects &#151; Results of Operations &#151; Research and Development Expenses&#148;. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">With the core CMOS and analog technologies in our portfolio, we are aggressively proceeding to miniaturization in line
with industry requirements. To differentiate our offering for higher value systems, we also seek to combine our core technologies with our specific knowhow and expertise, in particular in the area of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">System-in-Package.</FONT></FONT> </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our R&amp;D design centers offer a significant
advantage for us in quickly and cost effectively introducing products. In addition, we have advanced R&amp;D centers strategically located around the world, including in France, Italy, China, India, Singapore, the United Kingdom and the United
States. We have a technology council comprised of fifteen leading experts to review, evaluate and advise us on the competitive landscape. Our R&amp;D center in Greater Noida, India provides necessary support to the Group&#146;s design activities
worldwide and hosts R&amp;D activities focused on software development and core libraries development, with a strong emphasis on system solutions. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In 2008, we entered into an R&amp;D alliance with the International Semiconductor Development Alliance (&#147;ISDA&#148;) led by IBM, whose other core members are Samsung and Global Foundries, to develop
leading edge core CMOS technologies at <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">32/28-nm</FONT> and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">22/20-nm</FONT> nodes. In 2013, we extended our participation in ISDA to cover the next nodes
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(14/10/7-nm).</FONT> We are also working with the CEA Leti and IBM to develop in Crolles our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">FD-SOI</FONT> derivative technology, which, for the
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">28-nm</FONT> node and the next generation, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">14-nm,</FONT> are in development, and for the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">10-nm,</FONT> it is on our roadmap. This <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">FD-SOI</FONT> technology offers an alternative to the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Fin-FET</FONT> technology proposed by competitors for applications targeting low power dissipation. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In 2009, we also entered into a framework agreement with the French Ministry of Economy, Industry and Employment for the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;Nano-2012&#148;</FONT> Research and Development program. This program expired at the end of 2012. On July&nbsp;22, 2013, we announced the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Nano-2017</FONT> Research and Development
program. See &#147;Item&nbsp;5. Operating and Financial Review and Prospects &#151; Other Developments&#148;. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Furthermore, our manufacturing facility in Crolles, France houses a R&amp;D center, &#147;Centre Commun de
Microelectronique de Crolles&#148;. Laboratoire d&#146;Electronique de Technologie d&#146;Instrumentation, a research laboratory of CEA (one of our indirect shareholders), is our partner in this center. In 2012, a new structure,
</FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">28 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">
Institut de Recherche Technologique (&#147;IRT&#148;), was set up by CEA in the frame of the French initiative &#147;Investissements d&#146;Avenir&#148;. We participate in this program, which
takes place on CEA&#146;s premises, through investment and by contributing the expertise of some of our researchers. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">There can be no assurance that we will be able to generate the necessary funding to support the ongoing costs of our R&amp;D programs, or that we will be able to develop future technologies and
commercially implement them on satisfactory terms, or that our alliances will allow the successful development of
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">state-of-the-art</FONT></FONT></FONT> core CMOS or <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">FD-SOI</FONT> technologies on satisfactory terms and in line with
market requirements. See &#147;Item&nbsp;3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors &#151; Risks Related to Our Operations &#151; Our R&amp;D efforts are increasingly expensive and dependent on technology alliances, and our business, results of
operations and prospects could be materially adversely affected by the failure or termination of such alliances&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In Italy, our technology R&amp;D development activities occur principally in Agrate and Catania. In Agrate, such activities encompass prototyping, pilot and volume production of newly developed
technologies with the objective of accelerating process industrialization and time to market for Smart Power affiliation (BCD), including on SOI, High Voltage CMOS and MEMS. In addition, we plan to set up a 300-mm pilot line for manufacturing and
R&amp;D for advanced BCD technology. We also run a joint operation under a consortium agreement with Micron Technologies (&#147;Micron&#148;) in which we and Micron each manage our respective technology R&amp;D programs. In Catania, we develop new
technologies for power discretes, SICs and gallium arsenide. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our Advanced Systems Technology
(&#147;AST&#148;) organization, primarily located in Agrate, creates system knowledge that supports our SoC development. AST&#146;s objective is to develop the advanced architectures that will drive key strategic applications, including health care,
wireless and data security. AST&#146;s challenge is to combine the expertise and expectations of our customers, industrial and academic partners, our central R&amp;D&nbsp;teams and product segments to create a cohesive, practical vision that defines
the hardware, software and system integration knowledge that we will need in the next three&nbsp;to five&nbsp;years and the strategies required to master them. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We play leadership roles in numerous projects running under the European Union&#146;s IST (Information Society Technologies) programs. We also participate in joint European research programs, such as the
ITEA, the Cluster for Application and Technology Research in Europe on NanoElectronics (&#147;CATRENE&#148;), ARTEMIS and the European Nanoelectronics Initiative Advisory Council (&#147;ENIAC&#148;) programs. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Property, Plants and Equipment </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We currently operate 14 main manufacturing sites around the world. The table below sets forth certain information with respect to our current manufacturing facilities, products and technologies. <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Front-end</FONT> manufacturing facilities are fabs and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">back-end</FONT> facilities are assembly, packaging and final testing plants. </FONT></P>

<TD VALIGN="bottom" NOWRAP> <P STYLE="border-bottom:1px solid #000000;width:30pt" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="1"><B>Location</B></FONT></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>

<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Fab 2 in Ang Mo Kio is to be reduced essentially to Microfluidics products, while in Catania Fab 1 will be progressively
converted into 200-mm and merged with Fab 2. There will be consolidation of our back-end activity in China to Shenzhen (closure of Longgang). </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">30 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">At the end of 2013, our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT>
facilities had a total maximum capacity of approximately <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">130,000&nbsp;200-mm</FONT> equivalent wafer starts per week. The number of wafer starts per week varies from facility to facility and from period to period as
a result of changes in product mix. Our advanced <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">300-mm</FONT> wafer <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">pilot-line</FONT> fabrication facility in Crolles, France had an installed capacity of 3,600 wafers per week at
the end of 2013, and we plan to increase production to up to approximately 6,000 wafers per week as required by market conditions and within the framework of our R&amp;D <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Nano-2017</FONT> program. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We own all of our manufacturing facilities, but certain facilities (Muar-Malaysia, Shenzhen and Longgang-China, Toa Payoh
and Ang Mo Kio-Singapore) are built on land, which are the subject of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> leases. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We have historically subcontracted a portion of total manufacturing volumes to external suppliers. In 2013 we purchased approximately 9% from external foundries of our total silicon production. Our plan
is to extend sourcing of silicon from external foundries up to above 20% of our total needs. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">At
December&nbsp;31, 2013, we had approximately $163&nbsp;million in outstanding commitments for purchases of equipment and other assets for delivery in 2014. In 2013, our capital spending, net of proceeds, was $531&nbsp;million, above the
$476&nbsp;million registered in 2012. In the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">2011-2013</FONT> period the ratio of capital investment spending to revenues was about 8.6%. For more information, see &#147;Item&nbsp;5. Operating and Financial Review
and Prospects &#151; Financial Outlook: Capital Investment&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our manufacturing processes are highly
complex, require technologically advanced and costly equipment and are continuously being modified in an effort to improve yields and product performance. Impurities or other difficulties in the manufacturing process can lower yields, interrupt
production or result in losses of products in process. As system complexity has increased and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">sub-micron</FONT> technology has become more advanced, manufacturing tolerances have been reduced and requirements for
precision and excellence have become even more demanding. Although our increased manufacturing efficiency has been an important factor in our improved results of operations, we have from time to time experienced production difficulties that have
caused delivery delays and quality control problems, as is common in the semiconductor industry. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In the
second part of 2013, we experienced demand progressing at a pace lower than expected. Nonetheless we have been able to properly balance our fabs and plants loading versus the inventories evolution to ensure the sound level of their operational
performances. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">No assurance can be given that we will be able to increase manufacturing efficiencies in the
future to the same extent as in the past, or that we will not experience production difficulties and/or unsaturation in the future. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In addition, as is common in the semiconductor industry, we have from time to time experienced difficulty in ramping up production at new facilities or effecting transitions to new manufacturing processes
and, consequently, have suffered delays in product deliveries or reduced yields. There can be no assurance that we will not experience manufacturing problems in achieving acceptable yields, product delivery delays or interruptions in production in
the future as a result of, among other things, capacity constraints, production bottlenecks, construction delays, equipment failure or maintenance, ramping up production at new facilities, upgrading or expanding existing facilities, changing our
process technologies, or contamination or fires, storms, earthquakes or other acts of nature, any of which could result in a loss of future revenues. In addition, the development of larger fabrication facilities that require <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">state-of-the-art</FONT></FONT></FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">sub-micron</FONT> technology and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">larger-sized</FONT>
wafers has increased the potential for losses associated with production difficulties, imperfections or other causes of defects. In the event of an incident leading to an interruption of production at a fab, we may not be able to shift production to
other facilities on a timely basis, or our customers may decide to purchase products from other suppliers, and, in either case, the loss of revenues and the impact on our relationship with our customers could be significant. Our operating results
could also be adversely affected by the increase in our fixed costs and operating expenses related to increases in production capacity if revenues do not increase commensurately. Finally, in periods of high demand, we increase our reliance on
external contractors for foundry and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">back-end</FONT> service. Any failure to perform by such subcontractors could impact our relationship with our customers and could materially affect our results of operations.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Intellectual Property (IP) </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">IP rights that apply to our various products include patents, copyrights, trade secrets, trademarks and mask work rights. A mask work is the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">two-</FONT> or <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">three-dimensional</FONT> layout of an integrated circuit. We currently own approximately 16,000 patents and pending patent applications, corresponding to over 9,000 patent families (each patent family containing all
patents originating from the same invention), including 598 original new patent applications filed in 2013. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">31 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our success depends in part on our ability to obtain patents, licenses and
other IP rights covering our products and their design and manufacturing processes. To that end, we intend to continue to seek patents on our innovations in our circuit designs, manufacturing processes, packaging technology and system applications
as well as on industry standards and other inventions. The process of seeking patent protection can be long and expensive, and there can be no assurance that patents will issue from currently pending or future applications or that, if patents are
issued, they will be of sufficient scope or strength to provide meaningful protection or any commercial advantage to us. In addition, effective copyright and trade secret protection may be unavailable or limited in certain countries. Competitors may
also develop technologies that are protected by patents and other IP rights and therefore such technologies may be unavailable to us or available to us subject to adverse terms and conditions. Management believes that our IP represents valuable
assets and intends to protect our investment in technology by enforcing all of our IP rights. We have also set up a dedicated team actively seeking to optimize the value from our IP portfolio by the licensing of our design technology and other IP,
including patents. We have used our patent portfolio to enter into several broad patent <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">cross-licenses</FONT> with several major semiconductor companies enabling us to design, manufacture and sell semiconductor
products without fear of infringing patents held by such companies, and intend to continue to use our patent portfolio to enter into such patent <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">cross-licensing</FONT> agreements with industry participants on
favorable terms and conditions. As our sales increase compared to those of our competitors, the strength of our patent portfolio may not be sufficient to guarantee the conclusion or renewal of broad patent
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">cross-licenses</FONT> on terms that do not affect our results of operations. Furthermore, as a result of litigation, or to address our business needs, we may be required to take a license to third party IP rights
upon economically unfavorable terms and conditions, and possibly pay damages for prior use, and/or face an injunction or exclusion order, all of which could have a material adverse effect on our results of operations and ability to compete.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">From time to time, we are involved in IP litigation and infringement claims. See &#147;Item&nbsp;8. Financial
Information &#151; Legal Proceedings&#148;. In the event a third party IP claim were to prevail, our operations may be interrupted and we may incur costs and damages, which could have a material adverse effect on our results of operations, cash flow
and financial condition. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Finally, we have received from time to time, and may in the future receive
communications from competitors or other third parties alleging infringement of certain patents and other IP rights of others, which have been and may in the future be followed by litigation. Regardless of the validity or the successful assertion of
such claims, we may incur significant costs with respect to the defense thereof, which could have a material adverse effect on our results of operations, cash flow or financial condition. See &#147;Item&nbsp;3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors
&#151; Risks Related to Our Operations &#151; We depend on patents to protect our rights to our technology and may face claims of infringing the IP rights of others&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Backlog </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our sales are made primarily pursuant to standard
purchase orders that are generally booked from one&nbsp;to twelve&nbsp;months in advance of delivery. Quantities actually purchased by customers, as well as prices, are subject to variations between booking and delivery and, in some cases, to
cancellation due to changes in customer needs or industry conditions. During periods of economic slowdown and/or industry overcapacity and/or declining selling prices, customer orders are not generally made far in advance of the scheduled shipment
date. Such reduced lead time can reduce management&#146;s ability to forecast production levels and revenues. When the economy rebounds, our customers may strongly increase their demands, which can result in capacity constraints due to our inability
to match manufacturing capacity with such demand. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In addition, our sales are affected by seasonality, with
the first quarter generally showing lowest revenue levels in the year, and the third or fourth quarter historically generating higher amounts of revenues. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We also sell certain products to key customers pursuant to frame contracts. Frame contracts are annual contracts with customers setting forth quantities and prices on specific products that may be ordered
in the future. These contracts allow us to schedule production capacity in advance and allow customers to manage their inventory levels consistent with <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">just-in-time</FONT></FONT>
principles while shortening the cycle times required to produce ordered products. Orders under frame contracts are also subject to a high degree of volatility, because they reflect expected market conditions which may or may not materialize. Thus,
they are subject to risks of price reduction, order cancellation and modifications as to quantities actually ordered resulting in inventory <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">build-ups.</FONT> </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Furthermore, developing industry trends, including customers&#146; use of outsourcing and their deployment of new and
revised supply chain models, may reduce our ability to forecast changes in customer demand and may increase our financial requirements in terms of capital expenditures and inventory levels. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">32 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We entered 2013 with a backlog lower than we had compared to 2012, as a
result of a difficult industry environment. During 2013, our backlog declined, in particular in the second half, mainly reflecting the impact of the wind-down of the ST-Ericsson business. Excluding the Wireless product line, we entered 2014 with a
backlog similar to what we had entering 2013. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Competition </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Markets for our products are intensely competitive. While only a few companies compete with us in all of our product
lines, we face significant competition in each of them. We compete with major international semiconductor companies. Smaller niche companies are also increasing their participation in the semiconductor market, and semiconductor foundry companies
have expanded significantly, particularly in Asia. Competitors include manufacturers of standard semiconductors, ASICs and fully customized ICs, including both chip and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">board-level</FONT> products, as well as
customers who develop their own IC products and foundry operations. Some of our competitors are also our customers. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The primary international semiconductor companies that compete with us include Analog Devices, Atmel, Avago, Broadcom, Fairchild Semiconductor, Freescale Semiconductor, Infineon, Intel, International
Rectifier, InvenSense, Linear Technology, LSI Logic, Marvell, Maxim, MediaTek, Microchip Technology, Mstar, NXP Semiconductors, ON Semiconductor, Qualcomm, Renesas, ROHM Semiconductor, Samsung, Texas Instruments, Toshiba, TSMC and Vishay.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We compete in different product lines to various degrees on the basis of price, technical performance,
product features, product system compatibility, customized design, availability, quality and sales and technical support. In particular, standard products may involve greater risk of competitive pricing, inventory imbalances and severe market
fluctuations than differentiated products. Our ability to compete successfully depends on elements both within and outside our control, including successful and timely development of new products and manufacturing processes, product performance and
quality, manufacturing yields and product availability, customer service, pricing, industry trends and general economic trends. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Organizational Structure and History </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We are organized in a matrix structure with geographic regions interacting with product groups, both supported by shared technology and manufacturing operations and by central functions, designed to
enable us to be closer to our customers and to facilitate communication among the R&amp;D, production, marketing and sales organizations. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">While STMicroelectronics N.V. is the parent company, we also conduct our operations through service activities from our subsidiaries. We provide certain administrative, human resources, legal, treasury,
strategy, manufacturing, marketing and other overhead services to our consolidated subsidiaries pursuant to service agreements for which we recover the cost. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The following table lists our consolidated subsidiaries and our percentage ownership as of December&nbsp;31, 2013: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We participate in certain programs established by the EU, individual countries and local authorities in Europe (primarily
in France and Italy). Such funding is generally provided to encourage R&amp;D activities and capital investment, industrialization and the economic development of underdeveloped regions. These programs are partially supported by direct funding, tax
credits and specific loans <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(low-interest</FONT> financing). </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Public funding
in France, Italy and Europe generally is open to all companies, regardless of their ownership or country of incorporation. The EU has developed model contracts for R&amp;D funding that require beneficiaries to disclose the results to third parties
on reasonable terms. As disclosed, the conditions for receipt of government funding may include eligibility restrictions, approval by EU authorities, annual budget appropriations, compliance with European Commission regulations, as well as
specifications regarding objectives and results. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Some of our R&amp;D government funding contracts involve
advance payments that require us to justify our expenses after receipt of funds. Certain specific contracts (Crolles, Grenoble, Rousset and Tours, France and Catania, Italy) contain commitments to maintain a minimum level of employment and/or
investment during a certain amount of time. There could be penalties (i.e., a partial refund due to the government) if these objectives are not fulfilled. Other contracts contain penalties for late deliveries or for breach of contract, which may
result in repayment obligations. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The main programs for R&amp;D in which we are involved include: (i)&nbsp;the
Eureka CATRENE cooperative R&amp;D program (Cluster for Application and Technology Research in Europe on NanoElectronics); (ii)&nbsp;EU R&amp;D&nbsp;projects with FP7 (Seventh Frame Program) for Information and Communication Technology;
(iii)&nbsp;European Joint Technology Initiatives (JTI) such as ENIAC (European Nanoelectronics Initiative Advisory Committee) and ARTEMIS (Embedded Computing Systems Initiative) operated by Joint Undertakings formed by the European Union, some
member states and industry; and (iv)&nbsp;national or regional programs for R&amp;D and for industrialization in the electronics industries involving many companies and laboratories. The pan European programs cover a period of several years, while
national or regional programs in France and Italy are subject mostly to annual budget appropriation. We were awarded in 2012 two&nbsp;of the first of five projects under the ENIAC &#147;KET (Key Enabling Technologies) Pilot Lines&#148; frame,
recently launched in Europe. They are devoted respectively to specific MEMS technologies (based in Italy) and FD SOI technologies (based in France). At 2013 end, we were awarded 4 new projects in the same &#147;KET Pilot Lines&#148; frame, for
embedded <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-volatile</FONT> memories, diversified image sensors, design in <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">FD-SOI</FONT> technologies (all in France) and again MEMS (in Italy). </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In December&nbsp;2013, the European Commission formalized Horizon 2020, the European Union&#146;s new research and
innovation framework for 2014 through 2020, which also includes provisions to continue supporting the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">public-private</FONT> partnerships that existed under FP7. In particular, the new ECSEL (Electronic Components and
Systems for European Leadership) JTI will be supported to boost Europe&#146;s electronics manufacturing capabilities. ECSEL is a merger of the ARTEMIS initiative and the ENIAC initiative, and it also incorporates research and innovation on smart
systems under EPoSS. ECSEL is expected to start in early 2014&nbsp;and run for 10&nbsp;years. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I></I>In
Italy, there are some national funding programs established to support the new FIRST (<I>Fondo per gli Investimenti nella Ricerca Scientifica e Tecnologica</I>) that will groups previous funding regulations FIRB (<I>Fondo per gli Investimenti della
Ricerca di Base</I>, aimed to fund fundamental research), FAR (<I>Fondo per le Agevolazioni alla Ricerca</I>, to fund industrial research), and FSC (<I>Fondo per lo Sviluppo e la Coesione</I>) the FCS (<I>Fondo per la Competitivit&agrave; e lo
Sviluppo</I>). The FRI (<I>Fondo rotativo per il sostegno alle imprese e agli investimenti in ricerca</I>) funds research and innovation activities and the new FIT (<I>Fondo speciale rotativo per l&#146;Innovazione Tecnologica</I>) FCS (<I>Fondo per
la Crescita Sostenibile</I>) that is designed to fund precompetitive development in manufacturing. These programs are not limited to microelectronics and are intended to support industry R&amp;D in any segment. Italian programs often cover several
years and the approval phase is quite long, up to two&nbsp;or<I> </I></FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">35 </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>
</I>three&nbsp;years. In 2013, within the PON (<I>Programma Operativo Nazionale &#147;Ricerca e competitivit&agrave; 2007 2013&#148;</I>), the Italian Research Ministry finalized the complete
ranking of the approved proposals for the DTA (<I>Distretti ad Alta Tecnologia</I>), and seven projects involving the company were formalized and one of our proposals was selected for funding.<I> </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In Italy, according to the ARTEMIS and ENIAC Joint Undertaking procedures related to calls for proposals, in 2013 the
Italian Research Ministry approved public grants for an additional four ENIAC projects (of which one&nbsp;was under the ENIAC&#146;s call for &#147;Key Enabling Technologies &#151; Pilot Lines) and one&nbsp;for ARTEMIS project (under the ARTEMIS
Innovation Pilot Program call) involving the company. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Furthermore, there are some regional funding tools for
research that can be addressed by local initiatives, primarily in the regions of Puglia, Sicily, Campania and Val d&#146;Aosta, provided that a reasonable regional <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">socio-economic</FONT> impact could be recognized in
terms of industrial exploitation, new professional hiring and/or cooperation with local academia and public laboratories. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In 2006, the EU Commission allowed the modification of the conditions of a grant pertaining to the building, facilitation and equipment of our facility in Catania, Italy (the &#147;M6 Plant&#148;).
Following this decision, the authorized timeframe for completion of the project was extended and the Italian government was authorized to allocate &#128;446&nbsp;million, out of the &#128;542&nbsp;million grants originally authorized, for the
completion of the M6 Plant if we made a further investment of &#128;1,700&nbsp;million between January&nbsp;1, 2006 through the end of 2009. On the basis of the investments actually realized during the period, we recorded an amount of approximately
&#128;78&nbsp;million as funding for capital investment of which approximately &#128;44&nbsp;million has been received to date. On September&nbsp;13, 2011, the European Commission initiated a review of the M6 investment and related benefits,
requesting information from the Italian government about the status and the ownership of the benefits of the M6&nbsp;investment during the period <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">2001-2006.</FONT> The Italian authorities responded to all such
requests for information in 2011 and 2012 concerning primarily the history of the investment made, the motivation of the state aid granted, the formal interpretation related to the definition of &#147;investment activation&#148;, and its application
to the M6 case. To our knowledge, no proceedings are ongoing. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I></I>In France, support for R&amp;D is given
by public agencies such as ANR (<I>Agence Nationale de la Recherche</I>), or OSEO (the agency taking over the missions and budgets of the AII Agency for Industrial Innovation), generally for consortia of partners grouping universities, public
laboratories and private actors (large and small). The agencies operate via calls for project proposals, most often related to the identified &#147;clusters of competitiveness&#148; (<I>P&ocirc;les de Comp&eacute;titivit&eacute;</I>) throughout the
French territory. The most relevant for us are &#145;Minalogic&#146; around Grenoble, &#145;SCS&#146; in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">south-east</FONT> area covering Rousset and &#145;S2E2&#146; in the Tours area. The selected projects
receive a support limited to 25% or 35% of the actual R&amp;D expenses, depending on the type of project. The funding is given when technical reports have been accepted by the agencies; all expenses must be documented and financial audits are
organized by the agencies to check their eligibility.<I> </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Another important contribution is given by the
Ministry of Industry (&#147;FCE&#148;) and by local public authorities. Specific support for microelectronics is provided through FCE to all the companies with activities in France in the semiconductor industry. The amount of support under French
programs is decided annually and subject to budget appropriation. In 2012, we terminated the execution of the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;Nano-2012&#148;</FONT> Research and Development program, which is designed to promote the
development of advanced CMOS <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(32-nm</FONT> and below) technologies for system on chip semiconductor products in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Grenoble-Crolles</FONT> region of France, in cooperation with the
ISDA. In this program, STMicroelectronics (Crolles and Grenoble sites) was the leading contributor, with over 30 other partners (universities, public research laboratories, large groups and small companies (SMEs)). Under this frame agreement, an
overall funding budget of &#128;340&nbsp;million (about $450&nbsp;million) in grants was put in place for us for the period <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">2008-2012,</FONT> subject to the conclusion of agreements every year with the public
authorities (the French State being represented by the Ministry of Industry, and local authorities), and provided that all technical parameters and objectives are met. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Due to a major change in the taxation regime in France, the local authorities have received lower tax receipts than
before. During the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;Nano-2012&#148;</FONT> program, some of the local authorities involved have, as a result of such tax receipts, decided to suspend their funding obligations related to the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;Nano-2012&#148;</FONT> program. Therefore, the benefit for us and the other partners ended up being lower than expected as the French government did not agree to compensate us for the shortfall in support from the
local authorities. At the end of 2012, the program ended and a final review was completed in April&nbsp;2013. The final review concluded that the technical program had been fully executed in line with the plans, helping to further develop the
Grenoble ecosystem. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">36 </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">On July&nbsp;22, 2013, the French Prime Minister announced the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Nano-2017</FONT> Research and Development program, a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">five-year</FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">public-private</FONT> strategic R&amp;D program led by us to further advance our
leadership in key embedded processing solutions and technologies. The project draws support from a broad coalition of French national, regional and local authorities as well as by the European community through the ENIAC Joint Technology Initiative.
Funding for the program is subject to approval by the European Commission. Ultimately, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;Nano-2017&#148;</FONT> will strengthen our leadership in such key technologies as
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">FD-SOI</FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(low-power,</FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">high-performance</FONT> processing), <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">next-generation</FONT> imaging (sensors and
image signal processors), and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">next-generation</FONT> embedded <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-volatile</FONT> memories. These technologies are at the core of our embedded processing solutions which include
microcontrollers, imaging solutions, digital consumer products, application processors and digital ASICs. The <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">pan-European</FONT> enlargement of this program (with partners in close to 20&nbsp;European countries)
will also contribute to the strengthening of European cooperation in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">micro-nanoelectronics</FONT> sector, along the entire value chain, from materials and equipment to components and system design. This program
relies on leading industry clusters in Europe, such as Dresden (Germany), <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Leuven-Eindhoven</FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(Belgium-the</FONT> Netherlands) and
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Grenoble-Crolles</FONT> (France). While we expect to receive public funding under the Nano 2017 agreement in the course of the current quarter, there is no guarantee that the program will be approved or if it is
approved, that there will be no modifications that could negatively affect the R&amp;D program, all of which could have a material adverse effect on our results of operations. See &#147;Item 3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors &#151; Risks
Related to Our Operations &#151; If we fail to receive the necessary funding for our R&amp;D program, we may have to reconsider our strategy, which could adversely impact our results of operations&#148; and &#147;&#151; If we fail to meet the
condition and approval requirements applicable to public funding, which we have received in the past, we may face demands for repayment, which may increase our costs and impact our results of operations&#148;. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I></I>A new type of R&amp;D support program was set up in France in 2011, as part of a global rejuvenation effort aimed
at research and industry (&#147;Investissements d&#146;Avenir&#148; or IA). This program is coordinated by the CGI (<I>Commissariat G&eacute;n&eacute;ral aux Investissements d&#146;Avenir</I>) and targets industrial sectors of high relevance. We
have been granted three&nbsp;projects under this frame, which started in 2013: one for &#147;Tours 2015&#148; covering three&nbsp;types of technologies developed in cooperation with public laboratories, one for Rousset &#147;MAGE&#148; targeting the
development of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ultra-low</FONT> power secure microcontrollers and one in the area of electricity metering: <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;So-Grid&#148;.</FONT><I> </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We also benefit from tax credits for R&amp;D activities in several countries (notably in France). R&amp;D tax credits
consist of tax benefits granted to companies on an open and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-discriminatory</FONT> base for their research&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;development activities. See &#147;Item&nbsp;5. Operating and Financial Review and
Prospects &#151; Results of Operations &#151; Research and Development Expenses&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Funding for R&amp;D
activities is the most common form of funding that we receive. Public funding for R&amp;D is recorded as &#147;Other Income and Expenses, net&#148; in our Consolidated Statements of Income and booked pro rata in relation to the relevant cost once
the agreement with the respective government agency has been signed and all applicable conditions are met. See Note 2 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Government support for capital expenditures funding has been used to support our capital investment. Although receipt of these funds is not directly reflected in our results of operations, the resulting
lower amounts recorded in property, plant and equipment costs reduce the level of depreciation recognized by us. See Note&nbsp;2 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As a third category of government funding, we receive some loans, mainly related to large capital investment projects, at
preferential interest rates. See Note 13 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Funding of programs in
France and Italy is subject to annual appropriation, and if such governments or local authorities were unable to provide anticipated funding on a timely basis or if existing government or <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">local-authority-funded</FONT></FONT> programs were curtailed or discontinued, or if we were unable to fulfill our eligibility requirements, such an occurrence could have a material adverse effect on our business, operating
results and financial condition. Another reason for the delayed funding execution, after national approval, is the obligation European governments have to notify the European Commission DG Competition when their support exceeds
&#128;7.5&nbsp;million. From time to time, we have experienced delays in the receipt of funding under these programs. As the availability of such funding is substantially outside our control, there can be no assurance that we will continue to
benefit from such government support, that sufficient alternative funding would be available if necessary, or that any such alternative funding would be provided on terms as favorable to us as those previously committed. Due to changes in
legislation and/or review by the competent administrative or judicial bodies, there can be no assurance that government funding granted to us may not be revoked or challenged or discontinued, in whole or in part, by any competent state or European
authority, until the legal time period for challenging or </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">37 </FONT></P>
