<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE="margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Impairment, restructuring charges and other related closure costs</FONT></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">$</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(29</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">)&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">$</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(120</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">)&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">$</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(588</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">)&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In the fourth quarter of 2013, we recorded $29&nbsp;million of impairment, restructuring
charges and other related closure costs, of which: $20&nbsp;million restructuring charges related to our headcount reduction initiative targeting quarterly net operating expenses in the range of $600 to $650&nbsp;million by the beginning of 2014,
$5&nbsp;million impairment charge on Veredus as a result of the reclassification of its assets as Assets held for sale as of December&nbsp;31, 2013, $2&nbsp;million restructuring charges related to the manufacturing consolidation plans and
$2&nbsp;million related to other restructuring initiatives. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In the third quarter of 2013, we recorded
$120&nbsp;million of impairment, restructuring charges and other related closure costs, primarily consisting of: $56&nbsp;million recorded as impairment charges on the DCG goodwill and dedicated intangible assets following our yearly impairment
test, $35&nbsp;million recorded as impairment and restructuring charges related to the manufacturing consolidation plans and $22&nbsp;million recorded as restructuring charges related to our headcount reduction initiative targeting quarterly net
operating expenses in the range of $600 to $650&nbsp;million by the beginning of 2014. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In the fourth quarter
of 2012, we recorded $588&nbsp;million of impairment, restructuring charges and other related closure costs, of which: $544&nbsp;million was recorded as a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT> impairment on our Wireless goodwill and other
intangible assets, $20&nbsp;million was recorded in relation to our Digital restructuring plan announced in October&nbsp;2012 and was composed of employee termination benefits, as well as $7&nbsp;million impairment charges on intangible assets with
no future use, $16&nbsp;million was related to the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> restructuring plan announced in April&nbsp;2012, primarily consisting of employee termination benefits and $8&nbsp;million was related to other
restructuring initiatives. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B><I>Operating loss </I></B></FONT></P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The fourth quarter of 2013 registered an operating loss of $11&nbsp;million compared to
an operating loss of $66&nbsp;million in the prior quarter and an operating loss of $730&nbsp;million in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-ago</FONT> quarter. The fourth quarter of 2013 registered an improvement in our operating results,
both year-over-year and sequentially, driven by lower operating expenses and lower impairment and restructuring charges. </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">By product segment, SP&amp;A reported an increase in its operating income
compared to the prior quarter. Our EPS segment increased sequentially its operating losses, mainly due to the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> positive impact recorded in the third quarter 2013 for the sale of businesses. The segment
&#147;Others&#148; decreased its losses to $41&nbsp;million, from $123&nbsp;million in the prior quarter, mainly due to lower impairment and restructuring charges. On a
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-over-year</FONT></FONT> basis, our SP&amp;A segment operating income decreased from $106&nbsp;million in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-ago</FONT> quarter to
$96&nbsp;million while EPS improved its results from a loss of $182&nbsp;million to a loss of $66&nbsp;million. The segment &#147;Others&#148; significantly decreased its losses due to lower impairment and restructuring charges. </FONT></P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">During the fourth quarter of 2013, we registered an income tax expense of
$8&nbsp;million, reflecting actual tax charges and benefits in each jurisdiction as well as the true-up of tax provisions based upon the most updated visibility on open tax matters in several jurisdictions. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our tax rate is variable and depends on changes in the level of operating results within various local jurisdictions and
on changes in the applicable taxation rates of these jurisdictions, as well as changes in estimations of our tax provisions. Our income tax amounts and rates depend also on our loss <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">carry-forwards</FONT> and their
relevant valuation allowances, which are based on estimated projected plans and available tax planning strategies; in the case of material changes in these plans, the valuation allowances could be adjusted accordingly with an impact on our tax
charges. We currently enjoy certain tax benefits in some countries. Such benefits may not be available in the future due to changes in the local jurisdictions; our effective tax rate could be different in future periods and may increase in the
coming years. In addition, our yearly income tax charges include the estimated impact of provisions related to potential tax positions which have been considered uncertain. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT

<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(1.8</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">)%&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(7.1</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">)%&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(19.8</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">)%&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">For the fourth quarter of 2013, we reported a net loss of $36&nbsp;million, improving
sequentially and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-over-year.</FONT></FONT> </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Earnings per share for the fourth quarter of 2013 was $(0.04) compared to $(0.16) in the third quarter of 2013 and $(0.48) per share in the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-ago</FONT> quarter.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In the fourth quarter of 2013, the impact per share after tax of impairment, restructuring charges and other
related closure costs and other <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> items, a non U.S. GAAP measure, was estimated to be approximately $(0.03) per share, while in the third quarter of 2013, it was estimated to be approximately
$(0.13)&nbsp;per share. In the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">year-ago</FONT> quarter, the impact of impairment, restructuring charges and other related closure costs and other <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">one-time</FONT> items was estimated
to be approximately $(0.37)&nbsp;per share. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Changes in Exchange Rates </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our results of operations and financial condition can be significantly affected by material changes in the exchange rates
between the U.S. dollar and other currencies, particularly the Euro. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As a market rule, the reference currency
for the semiconductor industry is the U.S. dollar and the market prices of semiconductor products are mainly denominated in U.S. dollars. However, revenues for some of our products (primarily our dedicated products sold in Europe) are quoted in
currencies other than the U.S. dollar and as such are directly affected by fluctuations in the value of the U.S. dollar. As a result of currency variations, the appreciation of the Euro compared to the U.S. dollar could increase, in the <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term,</FONT> our level of revenues when reported in U.S. dollars. Revenues for all other products, which are either quoted in U.S. dollars and billed in U.S. dollars or in local currencies for payment, tend not to be
affected significantly by fluctuations in exchange rates, except to the extent that there is a lag between the changes in currency rates and the adjustments in the local currency equivalent of the price paid for such products. Furthermore, certain
significant costs incurred by us, such as manufacturing costs, SG&amp;A expenses, and R&amp;D expenses, are largely incurred in the currency of the jurisdictions in which our operations are located. Given that most of our operations are located in
the Euro zone and other non U.S. dollar currency areas, including Singapore, our costs tend to increase when translated into U.S. dollars when the dollar weakens or to decrease when the U.S. dollar strengthens. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In summary, as our reporting currency is the U.S. dollar, exchange rate fluctuations affect our results of operations: in
particular, if the U.S. dollar weakens, our results are negatively impacted since we receive a limited part of our revenues, and more importantly, we incur a significant part of our costs, in currencies other than the U.S. dollar. On the other hand,
our results are favorably impacted when the dollar strengthens. The impact on our accounts could therefore be material, in the case of a material variation of the U.S. dollar exchange rate. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our principal strategy to reduce the risks associated with exchange rate fluctuations has been to balance as much as
possible the proportion of sales to our customers denominated in U.S. dollars with the amount of materials, purchases and services from our suppliers denominated in U.S. dollars, thereby reducing the potential exchange rate impact of certain
variable costs relative to revenues. Moreover, in order to further reduce the exposure to U.S. dollar exchange fluctuations, we have hedged certain line items on our Consolidated Statements of Income, in particular with respect to a portion of the
costs of goods sold, most of the R&amp;D&nbsp;expenses and certain SG&amp;A expenses, located in the Euro zone, which we account for as cash flow hedging contracts. We use three&nbsp;different types of hedging contracts, consisting of forward
contracts, collars and options. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">66 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our Consolidated Statements of Income for 2013 included income and expense items translated
at the average U.S. dollar exchange rate for the period, plus the impact of the hedging contracts expiring during the period. Our effective average exchange rate for 2013 and 2012 was $1.31 for &#128;1.00. Our effective exchange rate was $1.34 for
&#128;1.00 for the fourth quarter of 2013 and $1.31 for &#128;1.00 for the third quarter of 2013, while it was $1.30 for &#128;1.00 for the fourth quarter of 2012. These effective exchange rates reflect the actual exchange rates combined with the
impact of cash flow hedging contracts that matured in the period. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The time horizon of our cash flow hedging
for manufacturing costs and operating expenses may run up to 24&nbsp;months, for a limited percentage of our exposure to the Euro and under certain currency market circumstances. As of December&nbsp;31, 2013, the outstanding hedged amounts were
&#128;660&nbsp;million to cover manufacturing costs and &#128;489&nbsp;million to cover operating expenses, at an average exchange rate of about $1.3671 to &#128;1.00 and $1.3626 to &#128;1.00 (considering the options and collars at strike),
maturing over the period from January&nbsp;2, 2014 to December&nbsp;4, 2014. As of December&nbsp;31, 2013, these outstanding hedging contracts and certain expiring contracts covering manufacturing expenses capitalized in inventory resulted in a
deferred profit of approximately $39&nbsp;million before tax, recorded in &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the Consolidated Statements of Equity, compared to a deferred profit of approximately $24&nbsp;million before tax
at December&nbsp;31, 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We also hedge certain manufacturing costs denominated in Singapore dollars (SGD);
as of December&nbsp;31, 2013, the outstanding hedged amounts were SGD 149&nbsp;million at an average exchange rate of about SGD 1.2540 to $1.00 maturing over the period from January&nbsp;9, 2014 to December&nbsp;4, 2014. As of December&nbsp;31,
2013, these outstanding hedging contracts and certain expiring contracts covering manufacturing expenses capitalized in inventory resulted in a deferred loss of approximately $1 million before tax, recorded in &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive
income (loss)&#148; in the Consolidated Statements of Equity. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our cash flow hedging policy is not intended to
cover our full exposure and is based on hedging a portion of our exposure in the next four quarters and a declining percentage of our exposure in each quarter thereafter. In 2013, as a result of our cash flow hedging, we recorded a net profit of
$33&nbsp;million, consisting of a profit of about $14 million to R&amp;D expenses, a profit of about $16&nbsp;million to costs of goods sold and a profit of $3 million to SG&amp;A expenses, while in 2012, we recorded a net loss of $71&nbsp;million.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In addition to our cash flow hedging, in order to mitigate potential exchange rate risks on our commercial
transactions, we purchase and enter into forward foreign currency exchange contracts and currency options to cover foreign currency exposure in payables or receivables at our affiliates, which we account for as fair value instruments. We may in the
future purchase or sell similar types of instruments. See &#147;Item&nbsp;11. Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;. Furthermore, we may not predict in a timely fashion the amount of future transactions in the volatile
industry environment. No assurance may be given that our hedging activities will sufficiently protect us against declines in the value of the U.S. dollar. Consequently, our results of operations have been and may continue to be impacted by
fluctuations in exchange rates. The net effect of our consolidated foreign exchange exposure resulted in a net gain of $8&nbsp;million recorded in &#147;Other income and expenses, net&#148; in our 2013 Consolidated Statement of Income compared to a
net gain of $5&nbsp;million recorded in 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The assets and liabilities of subsidiaries are, for
consolidation purposes, translated into U.S. dollars at the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">period-end</FONT> exchange rate. Income and expenses, as well as cash flows, are translated at the average exchange rate for the period. The balance sheet
impact, as well as the income statement and cash flow impact, of such translations have been, and may be expected to be, significant from period to period since a large part of our assets and liabilities and activities are accounted for in Euros as
they are located in jurisdictions where the Euro is the functional currency. Adjustments resulting from the translation are recorded directly in equity, and are shown as &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the Consolidated
Statements of Equity. At December&nbsp;31, 2013, our outstanding indebtedness was denominated mainly in U.S. dollars and in Euros. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">For a more detailed discussion, see &#147;Item&nbsp;3. Key Information &#151; Risk Factors &#151; Risks Related to Our Operations&#148;. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Changes in Interest Rates </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Interest rates may fluctuate upon changes in financial market conditions and material changes can affect our results of operations and financial condition, since these changes can impact the total
interest income received on our cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities, as well as the total interest expense paid on our financial debt. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">67 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our interest income (expense), net, as reported in our Consolidated
Statements of Income, is the balance between interest income received from our cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities investments and interest expense paid on our financial liabilities (including the sale without recourse of
receivables) and bank fees (including fees on committed credit lines). Our interest income is dependent upon fluctuations in interest rates, mainly in U.S. dollars and Euros, since we invest primarily on a
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> basis; any increase or decrease in the market interest rates would mean an equivalent increase or decrease in our interest income. Our interest expenses are also dependent upon fluctuations in
interest rates, since our financial liabilities mainly consist of European Investment Bank Floating Rate Loans at Libor and Euribor plus variable spreads. Our 2016 Convertible Bond was fully redeemed in the second quarter of 2012. On March&nbsp;17,
2013, we repaid with available cash the residual Euro 350&nbsp;million outstanding 2013 Senior Bonds with a principal amount at issuance of Euro 500&nbsp;million. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">At December&nbsp;31, 2013, our total financial resources, including cash and cash equivalents and marketable securities,
generated an average interest income rate of 0.28%. At the same date, the average interest rate on our outstanding debt was 0.90%. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Changes in Equity Prices </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As of December&nbsp;31,
2013, we did not hold any significant equity participations, which could be subject to a material impact in changes in equity prices. However, we hold equity participations whose carrying value could be reduced due to further losses or impairment
charges of our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">equity-method</FONT> investments. See Note&nbsp;10 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Liquidity and Capital Resources </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Treasury activities are
regulated by our policies, which define procedures, objectives and controls. The policies focus on the management of our financial risk in terms of exposure to currency rates and interest rates. Most treasury activities are centralized, with any
local treasury activities subject to oversight from our head treasury office. The majority of our cash and cash equivalents are held in U.S. dollars and Euros and are placed with financial institutions rated at least a single A <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> rating, meaning at least A3 from Moody&#146;s Investor Service (&#147;Moody&#146;s&#148;) and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">A-</FONT> from Standard&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Poor&#146;s (&#147;S&amp;P&#148;) or
Fitch Ratings (&#147;Fitch&#148;), or better. Marginal amounts are held in other currencies. See &#147;Item&nbsp;11. Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our total liquidity and capital resources were $1,894&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2013, decreasing compared to
$2,493&nbsp;million at December&nbsp;31, 2012. As of December&nbsp;31, 2013, our total liquidity and capital resources were comprised of $1,836&nbsp;million in cash and cash equivalents, $1&nbsp;million in
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> deposits and $57&nbsp;million in marketable securities, all considered as current assets. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As of December&nbsp;31, 2013, marketable securities were $57&nbsp;million invested in senior debt securities at floating rate issued by primary financial institutions with an average rating of <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Baa2/A-/A</FONT> from Moody&#146;s, S&amp;P&nbsp;and Fitch, respectively. The Floating Rate Notes are classified as <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">available-for-sale</FONT></FONT> and
reported at fair value, with changes in fair value recognized as a separate component of &#147;Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss)&#148; in the Consolidated Statements of Equity, except if deemed to be <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">other-than-temporary.</FONT></FONT> Since the duration of the marketable securities portfolio was 0.05&nbsp;year, the value of the securities as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 corresponded to par value. As such, the cumulative
change in the fair value of our marketable securities portfolio was not material as of December&nbsp;31, 2013. The fair value of these securities is based on market prices publicly available through major financial information providers. The market
price of the marketable securities is influenced by changes in the credit standing of the issuer but is not significantly impacted by movement in interest rates. In 2013, we reported proceeds of $184&nbsp;million pursuant to sold or matured treasury
bills and Floating Rate Notes. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In 2011, we received cash proceeds of $356.8 million from Credit Suisse as the
full and final payment for the settlement of all outstanding litigation concerning Auction Rate Securities. Upon receipt of the funds, the ownership of the whole portfolio was transferred to Credit Suisse. We booked a pretax gain of approximately
$329 million in 2011 as a result of the settlement, out of which $6 million was reported on the line &#147;selling, general and administrative&#148; and $323 million as a realized gain on financial assets. This $356.8 million plus the $75 million
already cashed in made a total amount of $431.8 million that exceeded all losses and costs associated with the litigation. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">68 </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Liquidity </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We maintain a significant cash position and a low
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">debt-to-equity</FONT></FONT> ratio, which provide us with adequate financial flexibility. As in the past, our cash management policy is to finance our investment needs mainly with net
cash generated from operating activities. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">During 2013, our net cash decreased by $414&nbsp;million, due to
the net cash used in investing and financing activities exceeding the net cash from operating activities, primarily due to the repayment of the residual outstanding 2013 Senior Bonds in the amount of $455&nbsp;million, net payments for tangible
assets and the dividend payments. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">The components of our cash flow for the last three&nbsp;years are set forth
below: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>$</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>(414</B></FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>)&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>$</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>338</B></FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>&nbsp;&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>

<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>$</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>20</B></FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>&nbsp;&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net cash from operating activities</I>. The net cash from operating activities in 2013
was $366&nbsp;million, decreasing compared to $612&nbsp;million in the prior year period. Net cash from operating activities is the sum of (i)&nbsp;net income (loss) adjusted for <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT> items and
(ii)&nbsp;changes in assets and liabilities. The decrease in net cash from operating activities in 2013 compared to 2012 resulted from the less favorable changes in assets and liabilities, which was partially balanced by the net loss adjusted for <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT> items: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:6px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="5%"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="2%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&#149;</FONT></P></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Net income (loss) adjusted for <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cash</FONT> items increased to $333&nbsp;million of cash generated in 2013
compared to $109&nbsp;million in the prior year period, mainly due to the improved operating results; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="font-size:6px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="5%"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="2%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&#149;</FONT></P></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Changes in assets and liabilities generated cash for a total amount of $33<B>&nbsp;</B>million in 2013,&nbsp;compared to $503&nbsp;million of cash
generated in the prior year period, mainly due to a negative change in trade payables ($139&nbsp;million), trade receivables ($57&nbsp;million) and inventories ($22&nbsp;million). In 2012, changes were positive, mainly associated with a favorable
variation in inventories ($191&nbsp;million) and trade payables ($148&nbsp;million). Furthermore, the negative trend in trade receivables also included an unfavorable net cash impact of $72&nbsp;million, deriving from the sales, with no recourse, of
trade and other receivables, compared to a favorable $26&nbsp;million in 2012. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net cash
used in investing activities</I>. Investing activities used $379&nbsp;million of cash in 2013, mainly due to payments for the purchase of tangible assets and for intangible and financial assets, partially offset by the proceeds from the sale of
marketable securities. Payments for purchase of tangible assets, net of proceeds, totaled $531&nbsp;million, compared to $476&nbsp;million in 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net cash from </I>(<I>used in</I>)<I> financing activities</I>. Net cash used in financing activities was $388&nbsp;million in 2013 compared to $135&nbsp;million generated in 2012, mainly due to the
$455&nbsp;million repayment of the residual outstanding 2013 Senior Bonds. The financing activities in 2013 included $346&nbsp;million in dividends paid to stockholders, compared to $355&nbsp;million paid in 2012. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Free Cash Flow </I>(<I>non U.S. GAAP measure</I>). We also present Free Cash Flow, which is a non U.S.&nbsp;GAAP
measure, defined as (i)&nbsp;net cash from operating activities plus (ii)&nbsp;net cash used in investing activities, excluding payment for purchases (and proceeds from the sale) of marketable securities,
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> deposits and restricted cash, which are considered as temporary financial investments. The result of this definition is ultimately net cash from operating activities plus payment for purchase and
proceeds from sale of tangible, intangible and financial assets, proceeds received in sale of businesses, payment for business acquisitions, net proceeds from sale of stock received on investment divestiture and payment for funding of joint ventures
liquidation. We believe Free Cash Flow, a non U.S. GAAP measure, provides useful information for investors and management because it measures our capacity to generate cash from our operating and investing activities to sustain our operations. Free
Cash Flow is not a U.S. GAAP measure and does not represent total cash flow since it does not include the cash flows generated by or used in financing activities. Free Cash Flow reconciles with the total cash flow and the net cash increase
(decrease) by including the payment for purchases (and proceeds from the sale) of marketable securities, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> deposits, restricted cash and net cash from joint ventures deconsolidation, the net cash from
(used in) </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">69 </FONT></P>

<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Free Cash Flow was negative $179&nbsp;million in 2013, deteriorating compared to positive $33&nbsp;million in 2012,
primarily due to lower cash generated from operating activities. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Capital Resources </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Net Financial Position </I>(<I>non U.S. GAAP measure</I>).<I> </I>Our Net Financial Position represents the balance
between our total financial resources and our total financial debt. Our total financial resources include cash and cash equivalents, marketable securities, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> deposits and restricted cash, and our
total financial debt includes bank overdrafts, <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> debt and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt, as represented in our Consolidated Balance Sheets. Net Financial Position is not a
U.S. GAAP measure but we believe it provides useful information for investors because it gives evidence of our global position either in terms of net indebtedness or net cash by measuring our capital resources based on cash and cash equivalents and
marketable securities and the total level of our financial indebtedness, which included the 50% of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> indebtedness up to September&nbsp;1, 2013 when we deconsolidated
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson.</FONT> Consequently, we have in prior periods presented the Net Financial Position attributable to ST (&#147;ST Net Financial Position&#148;), which did not include the
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> indebtedness towards Ericsson, our partner in ST-Ericsson JVS. Our Net Financial Position for each period has been determined as follows from our Consolidated Balance Sheets: </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">70 </FONT></P>

<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">At December&nbsp;31, 2013, our financial debt was $1,153&nbsp;million,
composed of (i)&nbsp;$225&nbsp;million of current portion of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt and (ii)&nbsp;$928&nbsp;million of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt. The breakdown of our total financial debt
included: (i)&nbsp;$1,132&nbsp;million in European Investment Bank loans (the &#147;EIB Loans&#148;), (ii)&nbsp;$20&nbsp;million in loans from other funding programs, and (iii)&nbsp;$1&nbsp;million of capital leases. The EIB Loans are comprised of <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">four&nbsp;long-term</FONT> amortizing credit facilities as part of our R&amp;D funding programs. The first for R&amp;D in France was drawn in U.S. dollars from 2006 to 2008 for a total amount of $341&nbsp;million, of which
$97&nbsp;million remained outstanding as at December&nbsp;31, 2013. The second for R&amp;D projects in Italy, was drawn in U.S. dollars in 2008&nbsp;for a total amount of $380&nbsp;million, of which $163&nbsp;million remained outstanding as of
December&nbsp;31, 2013. The third, signed in 2010, is a &#128;350&nbsp;million <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">multi-currency</FONT> loan to support our industrial and R&amp;D programs. It was drawn mainly in U.S. dollars for an amount of
$321&nbsp;million and only partially in Euros for an amount of &#128;100&nbsp;million, of which $401&nbsp;million remained outstanding as of December&nbsp;31, 2013. The fourth, signed in the first quarter of 2013, is a &#128;350&nbsp;million
multicurrency loan which also supports our R&amp;D programs. It was drawn in U.S. dollars for an amount of $471&nbsp;million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Additionally, we had unutilized committed <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">medium-term</FONT> credit facilities with core relationship banks of $730&nbsp;million. At December&nbsp;31, 2013, the amounts
available under our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">short-term</FONT> lines of credit were unutilized. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt contains standard conditions, but does not impose minimum financial ratios. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As of December&nbsp;31, 2013, debt payments due by year were as follows: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">$</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">292</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In February 2006, we issued $1,131 million principal amount at maturity zero coupon
senior convertible bonds due in February 2016. The bonds were convertible by the holder at any time prior to maturity at a conversion rate of 43.833898 shares per one thousand dollar face value of the bonds corresponding to 42,694,216 equivalent
shares. In order to optimize our liability management and yield, we repurchased a portion of our 2016 Convertible Bonds during 2009 (98,000 bonds for a total cash consideration of $103 million and corresponding to 4,295,722 shares) and in 2010
(385,830 bonds for a total cash consideration of $410 million and corresponding to 16,912,433 shares). On February&nbsp;23, 2011, certain holders redeemed 41,123 convertible bonds at a price of $1,077.58, out of the total of 490,170 outstanding
bonds, or about 8%. In the third and fourth quarters of 2011, we repurchased 248,645 bonds for a total cash consideration of $270 million, corresponding to 10,899,080 shares. On February&nbsp;23, 2012, certain holders redeemed 190,131 convertible
bonds at a price of $1,093.81, out of the total of 200,402 outstanding bonds, representing approximately 95% of the then outstanding convertible bonds. In addition, on March&nbsp;12, 2012, we accepted the further put of 4,980 bonds for a cash
consideration of $5&nbsp;million. On March&nbsp;28, 2012, we published a notice of sweep up redemption for the remaining 5,291 bonds outstanding, which were redeemed on May&nbsp;10, 2012. As of December&nbsp;31, 2013, there were no bonds remaining
outstanding. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">In March 2006, STMicroelectronics Finance B.V. (&#147;ST BV&#148;), a wholly owned subsidiary,
issued the 2013 Senior Bonds. These bonds, which matured on March&nbsp;17, 2013, paid a quarterly coupon rate of the three-month Euribor plus 0.40%. On March&nbsp;17, 2013, we repaid at maturity with available cash the residual outstanding 2013
Senior Bonds in the amount of $455 million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">On December&nbsp;19, 2013, Moody&#146;s lowered our senior debt
rating from &#147;Baa2&#148; to &#147;Baa3&#148; with stable outlook. On December&nbsp;18, 2012, S&amp;P lowered our senior debt rating from &#147;BBB+&#148; to &#147;BBB&#148; with negative outlook. We are also rated
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">&#147;BBB-&#148;</FONT> from Fitch on an unsolicited basis. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">71 </FONT></P>

<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(1)</FONT></P></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Contingent liabilities which cannot be quantified are excluded from the table above. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>

<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(2)</FONT></P></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Items not reflected on the Consolidated Balance Sheet at December&nbsp;31, 2013. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(3)</FONT></P></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Items reflected on the Consolidated Balance Sheet at December&nbsp;31, 2013. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(4)</FONT></P></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">See Note 13 to our Consolidated Financial Statements at December&nbsp;31, 2013 for additional information related to
<FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> debt. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">As a result of our planned closures
of certain manufacturing facilities, some of the aforementioned contracts have been terminated. The termination fees for the sites still in operation have not been taken into account. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Operating leases are mainly related to building leases and to equipment. The amount disclosed is composed of minimum
payments for future leases from 2014 to 2018 and thereafter. We lease land, buildings, plants and equipment under operating leases that expire at various dates under <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">non-cancelable</FONT> lease agreements.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Purchase obligations are primarily comprised of purchase commitments for equipment, for outsourced foundry
wafers and for software licenses. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Other obligations primarily relate to firm contractual commitments with
respect to partnership and cooperation agreements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Long-term</FONT> debt
obligations mainly consist of bank loans. In 2014, we expect to redeem with available cash and cash equivalents a $225&nbsp;million loan received from European Investment Bank as an annual installment. See &#147;&#151; Net financial position (non
U.S. GAAP measure)&#148; above. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Pension obligations amounting to $369&nbsp;million consist of our best
estimates of the amounts projected to be payable by us for the pension and <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">post-employment</FONT> plans. The final actual amount to be paid and related timing of such payments may vary significantly due to early
retirements, terminations and changes in assumptions rates. See Note 14 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. As part of the Flash divestiture, we retained the obligation to fund the severance payment (<I>trattamento di fine rapporto</I>) due to
certain transferred employees by the defined amount of about $17&nbsp;million which qualifies as a defined benefit plan and was classified as an &#147;other <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> liability&#148; at December&nbsp;31, 2013.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Other <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> liabilities include, future obligations related to
our restructuring plans and miscellaneous contractual obligations. They also include at December&nbsp;31, 2013, following the Flash divestiture in 2008, a <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">long-term</FONT> liability for capacity rights amounting to
$4&nbsp;million. In accordance with the authoritative guidance for accounting for uncertainty in income taxes, as of December&nbsp;31, 2013, we had unrecognized tax benefits of $255&nbsp;million. We do not expect to recognize any of these tax
benefits in 2014. We are not, however, able to provide a reasonably reliable estimate of when these benefits will be recognized. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Off-Balance</FONT> Sheet Arrangements </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We had no material <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">off-balance</FONT> sheet arrangements at December&nbsp;31, 2013. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">72 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Financial Outlook: Capital Investment </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our policy is to modulate our capital spending according to the evolution of the semiconductor market. Based on current
visibility on demand, we anticipate our capital expenditure to be approximately <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">$510-550&nbsp;million</FONT> in 2014, to be adjusted based on demand thereafter. The most important of our 2014 capital expenditure
projects are expected to be: (a)&nbsp;for our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT> facilities: (i)&nbsp;in our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">300-mm</FONT> fab in Crolles, technology evolution to consolidate the capability for <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">20-nm</FONT> processes and mix evolution to support the production ramp up of new technologies for microcontrollers and automotive advanced products; (ii)&nbsp;a few selective programs of mix evolution, mainly in the area
of analog processes; (iii)&nbsp;qualification of technologies in 200-mm in Singapore and Catania; and (iv)&nbsp;quality, safety, maintenance, and productivity and cost savings investments in both <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">150-mm</FONT> and <FONT
STYLE="white-space:nowrap">200-mm</FONT> <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">front-end</FONT> fabs; (b)&nbsp;for our <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">back-end</FONT> facilities, capital expenditures will mainly be dedicated to: (i)&nbsp;capacity growth
on certain package families, to sustain market demand; (ii)&nbsp;modernization of package lines targeting cost savings benefits; and (iii)&nbsp;specific investments in the areas of factory automation, quality, environment and energy savings; and
(c)&nbsp;an overall capacity adjustment in final testing and wafers probing (EWS) according to changes in demand. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We will continue to monitor our level of capital spending by taking into consideration factors such as trends in the semiconductor industry and capacity utilization. We expect to need significant
financial resources in the coming years for capital expenditures and for our investments in manufacturing and R&amp;D. We plan to fund our capital requirements from cash provided by operating activities, available funds and support from
third&nbsp;parties, and may have recourse to borrowings under available credit lines and, to the extent necessary or attractive based on market conditions prevailing at the time, the issuance of debt, convertible bonds or additional equity
securities. A substantial deterioration of our economic results, and consequently of our profitability, could generate a deterioration of the cash generated by our operating activities. Therefore, there can be no assurance that, in future periods,
we will generate the same level of cash as in prior years to fund our capital expenditure plans for expanding/upgrading our production facilities, our working capital requirements, our R&amp;D and manufacturing costs. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We have an equity investment in 3Sun. We are currently evaluating our strategy and multiple scenarios are being
considered. We currently foresee that there may be a need to provide additional financial resources to 3Sun. In the event of a withdrawal by one of our partners, our financial support could cover up to 50% of the required funding. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Furthermore, as a result of the exit from the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> joint venture, our
exposure is limited to covering 50% of <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">ST-Ericsson</FONT> needs to complete the <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">wind-down,</FONT> which are estimated in the range of $30&nbsp;to $40&nbsp;million for each partner.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">We believe that we have the financial resources needed to meet our currently projected business requirements
for the next twelve&nbsp;months, including capital expenditures for our manufacturing activities, working capital requirements, approved dividend payments and the repayment of our debts in line with their maturity dates. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Impact of Recently Issued U.S. Accounting Standards </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">See Note 2 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B><FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">Equity-method</FONT> investments </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">See Note 10 to our Consolidated Financial Statements. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Backlog and Customers </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">See &#147;Item&nbsp;4. Information on the Company &#151; Backlog&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:18px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B>Directors, Senior Management and Employees </B></FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT

Company is entrusted to the Managing Board under the supervision of the Supervisory Board. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B><I>Supervisory Board
</I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Our Supervisory Board advises our Managing Board and is responsible for supervising the policies
pursued by our Managing Board and the general course of our affairs and business. Our Supervisory Board consists of </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">73 </FONT></P>

<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
<TR BGCOLOR="#cceeff">
<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE="margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Tom de Waard</FONT></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Member</FONT></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">1998</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">2014</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">67</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="line-height:8px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:2px;border-bottom:0.5pt solid #000000;width:10%">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="4%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="justify"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">(1)</FONT></P></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was appointed as a member of our Supervisory Board on June&nbsp;21, 2013. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Resolutions of our Supervisory Board require the approval of at least three&nbsp;quarters of its members in office. Our
Supervisory Board must meet upon request by two&nbsp;or more of its members or by our Managing Board. Our Supervisory Board meets at least five times a year, including once per quarter to approve our quarterly and annual accounts and their release.
Our Supervisory Board has adopted a Supervisory Board Charter setting forth its duties, responsibilities and operations, as mentioned below. This charter is available on our website (www.st.com). </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Pursuant to Dutch law, there is no mandatory retirement age for members of our Supervisory Board. Members of the
Supervisory Board may be suspended or dismissed by our annual shareholders&#146; meeting. Our Supervisory Board may make a proposal to our annual shareholders&#146; meeting for the suspension or dismissal of one or more of its members. Each member
of our Supervisory Board must resign no later than three years after appointment, as described in our Articles of Association, but may be reappointed following the expiration of his/her term of office. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Biographies of our Current Supervisory Board Members </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Didier Lombard has been a member of our Supervisory Board since 2004 and has been its Chairman since May&nbsp;2011.
Mr.&nbsp;Lombard serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee, Strategic Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. He is the Chairman of both the Compensation Committee and the Strategic Committee.
Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was appointed Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Orange (formerly France Telecom) in March&nbsp;2005, and served as Chief Executive Officer until February&nbsp;2010 and Chairman until March&nbsp;2011. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard began his
career in the Research and Development division of Orange in 1967. From 1989 to 1990, he served as scientific and technological director at the Ministry of Research and Technology. From 1991 to 1998, he served as General Director for industrial
strategies at the French Ministry of Economy, Finances and Industry, and from 1999 to 2003 he served as an Ambassador at large for foreign investments in France and as President of the French Agency for International Investments. From 2003 through
February&nbsp;2005, he served as Orange&#146;s Senior Executive Vice President in charge of technologies, strategic partnerships and new usages and as a member of Orange&#146;s Executive Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard is also a member of the Board of
Directors of Thales and Technicolor (previously Thomson), one of our customers, as well as a member of the Supervisory Board of Radiall. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard was also a member until his resignation on November&nbsp;15, 2006 of the Supervisory Board of
ST Holding, our largest shareholder. Mr.&nbsp;Lombard is a graduate of the Ecole Polytechnique and the Ecole Nationale Sup&eacute;rieure des T&eacute;l&eacute;communications. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Bruno Steve has been a member of our Supervisory Board since 1989 and has been its Vice Chairman since May&nbsp;2011. He
has previously held the positions of Chairman and member. Mr.&nbsp;Steve serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee, Strategic Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. He was with Istituto per la Ricostruzione
Industriale IRI S.p.A. (&#147;I.R.I&#148;), a former shareholder of Finmeccanica, Finmeccanica and other affiliates of I.R.I. in various senior positions for over 17&nbsp;years. Mr.&nbsp;Steve served as Chairman of the Statutory Auditors of Selex
Galileo S.p.A. until December&nbsp;2012. He previously served as </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">74 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">
member of the Statutory Auditors of Pirelli Tyres S.p.A. Until December&nbsp;1999, he served as Chairman of MEI. He served as the Chief Operating Officer of Finmeccanica from 1988 to
July&nbsp;1997 and Chief Executive Officer from May&nbsp;1995 to July&nbsp;1997. He was Senior Vice President of Planning, Finance and Control of I.R.I. from 1984 to 1988. Prior to 1984, Mr.&nbsp;Steve served in several key executive positions at
Telecom Italia. Until December&nbsp;2012, he was also a professor at LUISS Guido Carli University in Rome. Mr.&nbsp;Steve was Vice Chairman from May&nbsp;1999 to March&nbsp;2002, Chairman from March&nbsp;2002 to May&nbsp;2003 and member until his
resignation on April&nbsp;21, 2004 of the Supervisory Board of ST Holding, our largest shareholder. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Jean
d&#146;Arthuys has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2011. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee, Strategic Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee.
Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys is also the Chairman and CEO of FT1CI. He joined Bpifrance (formerly Fonds Strat&eacute;gique d&#146;Investissement) in 2010 as Director and member of the Executive Committee. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys was a partner in the fund
PAI Partners from 2007 until 2010, in particular in charge of the sectors media, internet and telecom. He was previously Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of television channels Paris Premiere and W9. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys spent the main part
of his career at the Executive Board of the Group M6, where he had various functions (from 1996 until 2007). He managed in particular the activities of digital television and the development of the Group. He was a board member of TPS, Sportfive and
Newsweb. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys was also Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of the soccer club Girondins de Bordeaux. Mr.&nbsp;d&#146;Arthuys graduated from HEC Business School. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Janet G. Davidson has been a member of our Supervisory Board since June&nbsp;2013. She serves on our Supervisory
Board&#146;s Audit Committee and Strategic Committee. She began her career in 1979 as a member of the Technical Staff of Bell Laboratories, Lucent Technologies (as of 2006 Alcatel Lucent), and served from 1979 through 2011 in several key positions,
most recently as Chief Strategy Officer (2005 &#150; 2006), Chief Compliance Officer (2006 &#150; 2008) and EVP Quality&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Customer Care (2008 &#150; 2011). From 2005 through 2012, Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the Lehigh University
Board of Trustees. In 2007 she served on the Riverside Symphonia Board of Trustees and in 2005 and 2006, Ms.&nbsp;Davidson was a member of the Liberty Science Center Board of Trustees. Ms.&nbsp;Davidson is currently (since 2011) a member of the
board of the Alcatel Lucent Foundation. Ms.&nbsp;Davidson is a graduate of the Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech), Atlanta, GA, USA, and Lehigh University, Bethlehem, PA, USA and holds a Master&#146;s degree in Electrical Engineering.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Jean-Georges Malcor has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2011. Mr.&nbsp;Malcor serves on
our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee. He is the Chief Executive Officer of CGG. He is a graduate of Ecole Centrale de Paris. He also holds a Master of Sciences degree from Stanford University, and a Doctorat from Ecole des Mines.
Mr.&nbsp;Malcor began his career at the Thales group as an acoustic engineer in the Underwater Activities division where he was particularly in charge of hydrophone and geophone design and towed streamer programs. He then moved to the Sydney based
Thomson Sintra Pacific Australia, becoming Managing Director of the company in 1990. Back in France, he became Director of Marketing and Communications (1991), then Director, Foreign Operations of Thomson Sintra Activit&eacute;s Sous Marines (1993).
In 1996, he was appointed Managing Director of Thomson Marconi Sonar Australia which was, in addition to its military activities, the lead developing company for the solid geophysical streamer. In 1999, Mr.&nbsp;Malcor became the first Managing
Director of the newly formed joint venture Australian Defense Industry. During this time he operated the Sydney based Woolloomooloo Shipyard (the largest dry dock in the southern hemisphere). In 2002, he became Senior Vice President, International
Operations of Thales International. From 2004 to 2009, he was Senior Vice President in charge of the Naval Division, supervising all naval activities in Thales including ship design, building and maintenance. In January&nbsp;2009, he became Senior
Vice President, in charge of the Aerospace Division. In June&nbsp;2009, he moved to the position of Senior Vice President, Continental Europe, Turkey, Russia, Asia, Africa, Middle East, and Latin America. Mr.&nbsp;Malcor joined CGG in
January&nbsp;2010 as President and became CEO on June&nbsp;30, 2010. Since June&nbsp;2013, Mr.&nbsp;Malcor has been a member of the Supervisory Board (as well as its Appointment and Compensation Committee) of the Fives Group. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Alessandro Ovi was a member of our Supervisory Board from 1994 until his term expired at our Annual General Meeting of
Shareholders in March&nbsp;2005. He was reappointed to our Supervisory Board at the 2007 Annual General Meeting of Shareholders and served on the Strategic Committee and the Audit Committee until his term expired. He was reappointed to the
Supervisory Board on June&nbsp;21, 2013. Mr.&nbsp;Ovi serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and Strategic Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Ovi received a doctoral degree in Nuclear Engineering from the Politecnico in Milan and a Master&#146;s
Degree in Operations Research from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. He has been special advisor to the President of the European Community for five&nbsp;years and has served on the boards of Telecom Italia S.p.A, Finmeccanica S.p.A. and
Alitalia S.p.A. Currently, he is also a director of LandiRenzo S.p.A and Almaviva S.p.A. Mr.&nbsp;Ovi is a Life Trustee in Carnegie </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">75 </FONT></P>



<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">
Mellon University and a member of the board in the Italian Institute of Technology. Until April&nbsp;2000, he was the Chief Executive Officer of Tecnitel S.p.A., a subsidiary of Telecom Italia
Group. Prior to joining Tecnitel S.p.A., Mr.&nbsp;Ovi was the Senior Vice President of International Affairs and Communications at I.R.I. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Alessandro Rivera has been a member of our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2011. Mr.&nbsp;Rivera serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Compensation Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance
Committee. He has been the Head of Directorate IV &#147;Financial Sector Policy and Regulation Legal Affairs&#148; at the Department of the Treasury, Ministry of Economy and Finance, since 2008. He served as Head of Unit in the Department of the
Treasury from 2000 to 2008 and was responsible for a variety of policy matters: financial services and markets, banking foundations, accounting, finance, corporate governance and auditing. Since 2008, Mr.&nbsp;Rivera has been a Government
representative in the &#147;Consiglio Superiore&#148; of the Bank of Italy as well as serving on the board of directors and Compensation Committee of Cassa Depositi e Prestiti S.p.A. and Posta Italiana S.p.A., the Financial Services Committee and
the European Securities Committee. He was a member of the Accounting Regulatory Committee from 2002 to 2008 and a member of the Audit Regulatory Committee from 2005 to 2008. He served on the board of Italia Lavoro S.p.A. from 2005 to 2008 and was a
member of the Audit Committee and the Compensation Committee. Mr.&nbsp;Rivera was also the Chairman of the Audit Committee of the &#147;Fondo nazionale di garanzia degli intermediari finanziari&#148; (Italian investor compensation scheme) from 2003
to 2008. From 2001 to 2010, he was the Project Leader and Deputy Project Leader in several twinning projects with Eastern European Countries (the Russian Federation, the Czech Republic, Lithuania, and Bulgaria). He also served on the board of
Mediocredito del Friuli &#151; Venezia Giulia S.p.A from 2001 to 2003. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Martine Verluyten has been a member of
our Supervisory Board since May&nbsp;2012. Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten serves on our Supervisory Board&#146;s Audit Committee and has been its Chair since April&nbsp;22, 2013. Until 2011,&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten acted as CFO of Umicore N.V. based in
Brussels. Previously she was CFO of Mobistar N.V. <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(2001-2006),</FONT> having initially joined Mobistar in 2000 as Group Controller. She had earlier worked at Raychem since 1976, holding various management positions
during her 23&nbsp;year tenure, from Manager European Consolidations <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(1976-1979),</FONT> to General Accounting Manager based in the US <FONT STYLE="white-space:nowrap">(1979-1983).</FONT> She was then promoted to
Division Controller Telecom Division Europe from 1983 to 1990. In 1990, she was appointed Finance&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Administration Director back in Europe, then in 1995, Europe Controller Finance&nbsp;&amp;&nbsp;Administration Director until 1999.
Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten is also member of the board of directors of Thomas Cook plc, 3i plc and GBL (&#147;group Bruxelles Lambert&#148;). Ms.&nbsp;Verluyten began her career in 1973 at KPMG as an Auditor. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Tom de Waard has been a member of our Supervisory Board since 1998. Mr.&nbsp;de Waard serves on our Supervisory
Board&#146;s Audit Committee, Compensation Committee and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. He was Chairman of the Audit Committee from 1999 until 2013 and is also Chairman of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee.
Mr.&nbsp;de Waard was a partner at Clifford Chance, a leading international law firm, until October&nbsp;2011. From January&nbsp;1, 2005 to January&nbsp;1, 2007 he was a member of the Management Committee of Clifford Chance. Prior to joining
Clifford Chance, he was a partner at Stibbe, where he held several positions since 1971 and gained extensive experience working with major international companies, particularly with respect to corporate finance. He is a member of the Amsterdam bar
and was President of the Netherlands Bar Association from 1993 through 1995. He received his law degree from Leiden University in 1971. Mr.&nbsp;de Waard is the chairman of the Supervisory Board of BE Semiconductor Industries N.V. (&#147;BESI&#148;)
and a member of its Audit Compensation and Nominating Committees. Mr.&nbsp;de Waard is a member of the Supervisory Board of N.V. Nuon Energy and Chairman of its Compensation Committee. Mr.&nbsp;de&nbsp;Waard is Chairman of the Board of Stichting
Administratiekantoor aandelen Telegraaf Media Groep N.V. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><B><I>Supervisory Board Committees </I></B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:6px;margin-bottom:0px; text-indent:4%" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2"><I>Membership and Attendance</I>. As of December&nbsp;31, 2013, the composition of our Supervisory Board&#146;s committees
was as follows: (i)&nbsp;Ms.&nbsp;Martine Verluyten is the Chair of the Audit Committee, and Ms.&nbsp;Janet&nbsp;G.&nbsp;Davidson and Messrs. Jean-Georges Malcor, Alessandro Ovi and Tom de Waard are members of the Audit Committee;
(ii)&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Didier Lombard is the Chairman of the Compensation Committee, and Messrs.&nbsp;Jean d&#146;Arthuys, Alessandro Rivera, Bruno Steve and Tom de Waard are members of the Compensation Committee; (iii)&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Tom de Waard is the
Chairman of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee, and Messrs.&nbsp;Jean d&#146;Arthuys, Didier Lombard, Alessandro Rivera and Bruno Steve are members of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee; and (iv)&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Didier
Lombard is the Chairman of the Strategic Committee, and Ms.&nbsp;Janet G. Davidson and Messrs. Jean d&#146;Arthuys, Alessandro Ovi and Bruno Steve are members of the Strategic Committee. </FONT></P>

<TD VALIGN="top"> <P STYLE="margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em" ALIGN="justify"><FONT STYLE="font-family:Times New Roman" SIZE="2">Bruno Steve</FONT></P></TD>
